subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 17! In this unit you will become confident at multiplying and dividing larger numbers using efficient written methods. You already know your multiplication facts and you can multiply and divide small numbers in your head. Now you will learn how to handle 2-digit and 3-digit numbers by a 1-digit number quickly and accurately, using the column method for multiplication and an efficient method for division.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and work it through yourself, and only move on when the steps feel clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to set you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or mark your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you as you read. Multiplication and division are not spectator sports – you must do them, digit by digit, to truly learn them. Write the numbers neatly in columns, line up the place value carefully, and say each step out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly which step to practise next. Every skill in this unit rests on care and neatness, so slow, tidy work will serve you far better than rushing.
Use an efficient column method to multiply 2-digit and 3-digit numbers by a 1-digit number.
Use an efficient written method for division, including dividing with remainders.
Estimate products and quotients to check that your answers are reasonable.
Interpret remainders correctly in different real-world situations.
Solve word problems that involve multiplication and division.
Explain the relationship between multiplication and division, and use one to check the other.
Apply multiplication and division to multi-step problems.
Create and solve your own word problems for a given calculation.

concepts.title: The Column Method for Multiplication
concepts.explanation: Multiplication is a fast way of adding equal groups. If Halima has 5 baskets with 3 mangoes in each, she has 5 × 3 = 15 mangoes. The numbers being multiplied are called factors, and the answer is called the product. In 7 × 8 = 56, the factors are 7 and 8 and the product is 56. For small numbers you can use facts you already know. But when the numbers get larger – say 346 × 9 – you need an efficient written method. The column method lines the digits up by place value and multiplies one column at a time, working…
concepts.example: Let us work through 346 × 9 together, one careful step at a time.

concepts.title: Estimating Products and Multiplying with Zeros
concepts.explanation: Estimating before you calculate is a habit that good mathematicians keep for life. An estimate is a quick, rough answer using rounded numbers. It is not the real answer – it is a guard that warns you if your real answer is far too big or far too small. To estimate a product: round each number to something easy, multiply the rounded numbers, then compare. For example, to estimate 78 × 6, round 78 up to 80. Then 80 × 6 = 480, so 78 × 6 should be a little under 480 (because we rounded 78 up). The exact answer is 468…
concepts.example: Multiplying with zeros uses exactly the same column method, but you must be careful with place value. Take 205 × 4. Working right to left: 5 × 4 = 20, so write 0 and carry 2. Then the middle digit: 0 × 4 = 0, and adding…

concepts.title: An Efficient Method for Division
concepts.explanation: Division is sharing or grouping equally. There are two ways to picture it. Sharing: 15 ÷ 3 can mean sharing 15 dates equally among 3 children, giving 5 each. Grouping: 15 ÷ 3 can also mean making groups of 3 from 15 dates, giving 5 groups. Both give the same answer, 5. Division has its own special words. The number you are dividing is the dividend. The number you divide by is the divisor. The answer is the quotient, and anything left over is the remainder. In 17 ÷ 4 = 4 remainder 1, the 17 is the dividend, the 4…
concepts.example: For larger numbers we use an efficient written method often called short division, working from the left (the biggest place) towards the right. Let us divide 75 ÷ 4 step by step.

concepts.title: Interpreting Remainders
concepts.explanation: A remainder is the amount left over when a number does not divide exactly. The clever part is deciding what to do with it, because that depends entirely on the story of the problem. There are four common ways to handle a remainder. 1. Leave it as a whole number. If 25 children are put into teams of 4, then 25 ÷ 4 = 6 remainder 1, so you make 6 teams with 1 child left over. The remainder simply tells you how many are not in a full team.
concepts.example: 2. Round up. If you need at least 50 pencils and they come in packs of 6, then 50 ÷ 6 = 8 remainder 2. Eight packs give only 48, which is not enough, so you must buy 9 packs. When you need *enough* to cover everyone,…

concepts.title: Multiplication and Division Are Linked
concepts.explanation: Multiplication and division are inverse operations – each one undoes the other. Because 8 × 6 = 48, we also know 48 ÷ 6 = 8 and 48 ÷ 8 = 6. These four facts form a fact family. Knowing this link is powerful, because it lets you check a division by multiplying, and check a multiplication by dividing. To check a division, multiply the quotient by the divisor and add the remainder; you should get back the dividend. For example, check 75 ÷ 4 = 18 remainder 3: multiply 18 × 4 = 72, then add the remainder 72 + 3 = 75.…
concepts.example: The inverse link also helps with missing-number problems. If □ × 7 = 56, you can find the missing factor by dividing: 56 ÷ 7 = 8, so □ = 8. If 81 ÷ □ = 9, you can rearrange to □ × 9 = 81, so □ = 81 ÷ 9 = 9. Whenever a…

concepts.title: Choosing the Right Operation in Word Problems
concepts.explanation: The hardest part of a word problem is often deciding whether to multiply or divide. Here is a reliable guide. Multiply when you know the number of groups and how many are in each group, and you want the total. You are combining equal groups. Watch for words like *each*, *every*, *per*, *at* and *times*. Example: 28 boxes with 12 pencils in each box – multiply to find the total.
concepts.example: Divide when you know the total and either the number of groups or the size of each group, and you want to find the other. You are sharing equally or making equal groups. Watch for words like *share*, *split equally*,…

methods.title: How to multiply a 2- or 3-digit number by a 1-digit number
steps: Write the larger number on top, the 1-digit number below, lining up the ones.
Estimate the answer by rounding.
Multiply the ones; write the ones digit and carry the rest.
Multiply the tens, add the carried digit; write and carry.
Multiply the hundreds, add the carried digit; write the result.
Compare with your estimate to check it is reasonable.

methods.title: How to divide using short division
steps: Estimate the answer by rounding.
Divide the highest place value first.
Carry any remainder across to the next digit.
Continue until every digit is divided.
Write the quotient and any final remainder, then check by multiplying.

methods.title: How to interpret a remainder
steps: Read the story to see what the remainder means.
Leave it whole, round up, round down, or write it as a fraction – whichever fits.
Answer the question in a full sentence.

methods.title: Divide with a remainder
steps: Solution: Estimate: 60 ÷ 6 = 10.
Tens: 6 ÷ 6 = 1, no remainder, write 1.
Ones: 8 ÷ 6 = 1 remainder 2, write 1.
Answer: 11 remainder 2.

methods.title: Divide a 3-digit number
steps: Solution: Hundreds: 4 ÷ 9 = 0 remainder 4, carry 4 to make 43 tens.
Tens: 43 ÷ 9 = 4 remainder 7, write 4, carry 7 to make 78 ones.
Ones: 78 ÷ 9 = 8 remainder 6, write 8.
Answer: 48 remainder 6.

methods.title: Interpret a remainder (round up)
methods.example: A bus holds 36 children. 145 children go on a trip. How many buses are needed?
steps: Solution: 145 ÷ 36: four buses carry 4 × 36 = 144, leaving 1 child.
That last child still needs a seat, so round up to 5 buses.
Here the remainder means we need one more bus.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a 2-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate first: 80 × 6 = 480. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, add the carried 4 to get 46, write 46. Answer: 468, close to the estimate of 480.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a 3-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 500 × 7 = 3,500. Ones: 2 × 7 = 14, write 4, carry 1. Tens: 6 × 7 = 42, add 1 to get 43, write 3, carry 4. Hundreds: 4 × 7 = 28, add 4 to get 32, write 32. Answer: 3,234, near the estimate.

workedExamples.title: Multiply with a zero
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 3 × 7 = 21, write 1, carry 2. Tens: 0 × 7 = 0, add the carried 2 to get 2, write 2. Hundreds: 5 × 7 = 35, write 35. Answer: 3,521. The middle zero still needs its own column.

workedExamples.title: Divide with a remainder
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate: 60 ÷ 6 = 10. Tens: 6 ÷ 6 = 1, no remainder, write 1. Ones: 8 ÷ 6 = 1 remainder 2, write 1. Answer: 11 remainder 2. Check: 11 × 6 = 66, and 66 + 2 = 68. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Divide a 3-digit number
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Hundreds: 4 ÷ 9 = 0 remainder 4, carry 4 to make 43 tens. Tens: 43 ÷ 9 = 4 remainder 7, write 4, carry 7 to make 78 ones. Ones: 78 ÷ 9 = 8 remainder 6, write 8. Answer: 48 remainder 6. Check: 48 × 9 = 432, and 432 + 6 = 438. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Interpret a remainder (round up)
workedExamples.prompt: A bus holds 36 children. 145 children go on a trip. How many buses are needed?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 145 ÷ 36: four buses carry 4 × 36 = 144, leaving 1 child. That last child still needs a seat, so round up to 5 buses. Here the remainder means we need one more bus.

workedExamples.title: A multiplication word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A cinema has 28 rows with 6 seats in each row. How many seats are there in total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Equal groups, so multiply: 28 × 6. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 2 × 6 = 12, add 4 to get 16, write 16. Answer: 168 seats.

workedExamples.title: A two-step problem
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan has 8 fields and plants 45 trees in each. A storm damages 27 trees. How many healthy trees remain?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step 1, total trees: 8 × 45 = 360. Step 2, subtract the damaged trees: 360 − 27 = 333 healthy trees. Doing one step at a time keeps the working clear.

workedExamples.title: A missing-number problem
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use the inverse link. If 96 ÷ □ = 12, then □ × 12 = 96, so □ = 96 ÷ 12 = 8. Check: 96 ÷ 8 = 12. Whenever a number is hidden, look for a multiplication or division that reveals it.

workedExamples.title: A remainder as a fraction
workedExamples.prompt: Seven mangoes are shared equally among 3 children. How many does each child get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 7 ÷ 3 = 2 remainder 1. The single mango left over is split three ways, giving each child one-third more. So each child gets 2 and one-third mangoes. Because a mango can be cut up, the remainder becomes a fraction rather than being left whole.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing number
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing number: a) □ × 7 = 56 b) 81 ÷ □ = 9.

workedExamples.title: Find the missing digits
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing digits: a) 2□5 ÷ 5 = 43 b) 3□ × 4 = 124.
workedExamples.solution: 1) a) 215 ÷ 5 = 43, missing digit 1; b) 31 × 4 = 124, missing digit 1.

activities.title: Market Stall Multiplication
activities.materials: small stones or bottle caps as pretend shillings, paper price labels.
steps: Set up a pretend market stall with items priced at the same amount, for example mangoes at 7 shillings each.
Choose how many of one item a customer buys, such as 8 mangoes.
Work out the total cost with the column method, estimating first.
Pay with your stones and check the total is right. Swap roles and try new prices.
This activity shows how multiplication finds the total cost of equal-priced items every day at the market.

activities.title: Sharing Dates (Division and Remainders)
activities.materials: a handful of dates, beans or stones, and a few small bowls.
steps: Count out a number of dates, for example 43, and choose a number of bowls, for example 5.
Share the dates equally, one at a time, into the bowls.
Count how many are in each bowl and how many are left over – that leftover is the remainder.
Write the division: 43 ÷ 5 = 8 remainder 3. Try again with different numbers and predict the remainder first.

activities.title: Fact Family Triangles
steps: Draw a large triangle and write a product at the top corner, for example 48.
Write its two factors, 6 and 8, in the bottom corners.
Write all four facts in the family: 6 × 8 = 48, 8 × 6 = 48, 48 ÷ 6 = 8, 48 ÷ 8 = 6.
Make five more triangles and see how quickly you can write each fact family.
Fact families show clearly how multiplication and division are linked as inverse operations.

activities.title: Remainder Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a small pile of counters.
steps: Choose a divisor, for example 4.
Divide the numbers 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15 and 16 by 4 and record each remainder.
Look at the pattern of remainders: 2, 3, 0, 1, 2, 3, 0. What do you notice?
Predict: what is the largest remainder possible when dividing by 4? By 7? Check with counters.
You should discover that the remainder is always smaller than the divisor – a very useful rule.

activities.title: Estimation Challenge (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or a friend, paper and a pencil.
steps: One player writes a multiplication such as 287 × 6 or a division such as 438 ÷ 9.
Everyone quickly writes an estimate by rounding, before any exact working.
Now calculate the exact answer with a written method.
The player whose estimate is closest to the exact answer wins the round. Play several rounds.

activities.title: Design Your Own Word Problem
steps: Pick a calculation, for example 8 × 42 = 336 or 216 ÷ 6 = 36.
Choose a real context you know: livestock, market shopping, water collection, or a football match.
Write a clear word problem whose answer is your chosen calculation.
Give your problem to a family member or classmate to solve, then check their working together.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand remainders best? What was the trickiest part of the column method? Which do you find easier, multiplication or division, and why? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Column Multiplication Rule
rules.text: Line up place value and work from right to left: ones, then tens, then hundreds. If a column gives more than 9, write the ones digit and carry the tens to the next column. Add any carried digit before you write the next column.

rules.title: Division Rule
rules.text: Work from left to right: divide the biggest place first. Carry each remainder across to the next column. The remainder is always smaller than the divisor.

rules.title: Estimate and Check Rule
rules.text: Estimate first by rounding, so you can spot a silly answer. Check a division by multiplying the quotient by the divisor and adding the remainder.

terms: A number that is multiplied by another
The result of a division (not the remainder)
The amount left over after dividing
A sensible guess to check reasonableness
commonMistakes: Add the carry before writing that column
Give the zero its own multiply step
Round up to cover everyone; down for full groups
Groups and total known → multiply; total known → divide

realProblems.prompt: A factory near Kismaayo produces 156 toys each hour. How many toys does it produce in 7 hours?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A librarian in Hargeisa has 325 books to arrange on shelves that hold 25 books each. How many shelves are filled completely?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A group of 5 friends share 43 dates equally for iftar. How many does each friend get, and how many are left over?
realProblems.answer: 43 ÷ 5 = 8 remainder 3, so 8 dates each with 3 left over.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 43 ÷ 5 = 8 remainder 3, so 8 dates each with 3 left over.

realProblems.prompt: A baker makes 15 trays of 24 cookies, then sells 275 cookies. How many cookies are left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A school has 216 students divided into groups of 8. Each group needs 6 markers. How many markers are needed in total?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Two cars travel from Nairobi. The first goes 75 km per hour for 4 hours; the second goes 60 km per hour for 5 hours. Which car travels farther, and by how much?
realProblems.answer: Car 1: 75 × 4 = 300 km; Car 2: 60 × 5 = 300 km – they travel the same distance, so neither is farther.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Car 1: 75 × 4 = 300 km; Car 2: 60 × 5 = 300 km – they travel the same distance, so neither is farther.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the missing digits: a) 2□5 ÷ 5 = 43 b) 3□ × 4 = 124.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) a) 215 ÷ 5 = 43, missing digit 1; b) 31 × 4 = 124, missing digit 1.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Peng estimates 399 × 6 to be 2,400. Is that a good estimate? Work out the exact answer and explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 399 × 6 = 2,394, which is very close to 2,400, so it is a good estimate.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Ahmed says: multiply a number by 6 then divide by 3, and it is the same as multiplying by 2. Is he correct? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yes – multiplying by 6 then dividing by 3 is the same as multiplying by 6 ÷ 3 = 2; for example 5 × 6 = 30, 30 ÷ 3 = 10, and 5 × 2 = 10.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find a 2-digit number that gives a remainder of 3 when divided by 4 and a remainder of 2 when divided by 5.
What is the smallest 3-digit number that is divisible by both 6 and 8?
Explain the key idea in Choosing the Right Operation in Word Problems.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The hardest part of a word problem is often deciding whether to multiply or divide. Here is a reliable guide. Multiply when you know the number of groups and how many are in each group, and you want the total. You are combining equal groups. Watch for words like *each*, *every*, *per*, *at* and *times*. Example: 28 boxes with 12 pencils in each box – multiply to find the total.

questions.question: Calculate 78 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate first: 80 × 6 = 480. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, add the carried 4 to get 46, write 46. Answer: 468, close to the estimate of 480.

questions.question: Calculate 462 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Estimate: 500 × 7 = 3,500. Ones: 2 × 7 = 14, write 4, carry 1. Tens: 6 × 7 = 42, add 1 to get 43, write 3, carry 4. Hundreds: 4 × 7 = 28, add 4 to get 32, write 32. Answer: 3,234, near the estimate.

questions.question: Calculate 503 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 3 × 7 = 21, write 1, carry 2. Tens: 0 × 7 = 0, add the carried 2 to get 2, write 2. Hundreds: 5 × 7 = 35, write 35. Answer: 3,521. The middle zero still needs its own column.

questions.question: A cinema has 28 rows with 6 seats in each row. How many seats are there in total?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
questions.explanation: Equal groups, so multiply: 28 × 6. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 2 × 6 = 12, add 4 to get 16, write 16. Answer: 168 seats.

options: A number that is multiplied by another
questions.answer: A number that is multiplied by another

games.title: Quick Match: Factor

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
rounds.explanation: Estimate first: 80 × 6 = 480. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 7 × 6 = 42, add the carried 4 to get 46, write 46. Answer: 468, close to the estimate of 480.

rounds.prompt: A cinema has 28 rows with 6 seats in each row. How many seats are there in total?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 17 methods.
rounds.explanation: Equal groups, so multiply: 28 × 6. Ones: 8 × 6 = 48, write 8, carry 4. Tens: 2 × 6 = 12, add 4 to get 16, write 16. Answer: 168 seats.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and work it through yourself, and only move on when the steps feel clear. Your AI tutor is available day and night to set you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or mark your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you as you read. Multiplication and division are not spectator sports – you must do them, digit by digit, to truly learn them. Write the numbers neatly in columns, line up the place value carefully, and say each step out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it simply shows you exactly which step to practise next. Every skill in this unit rests on care and neatness, so slow, tidy work will serve you far better than rushing.
I can use an efficient column method to multiply 2-digit and 3-digit numbers by a 1-digit number.
I can use an efficient written method for division, including dividing with remainders.
I can estimate products and quotients to check that your answers are reasonable.
I can interpret remainders correctly in different real-world situations.
I can solve word problems that involve multiplication and division.
I can explain the relationship between multiplication and division, and use one to check the other.
